qa_pairs.question: What steps should a user take if graphics appear corrupted in an Ansys Workbench application launched from a Jupyterhub Desktop session without a GPU?
qa_pairs.answer: To address corrupted graphics in an Ansys Workbench application on a Jupyterhub Desktop when no GPU is requested, you need to create a custom desktop icon configured for Mesa mode. First, open a terminal window and navigate to your `~/Desktop` directory. Copy the existing `workbench.desktop` file to a new one named `workbench-mesa.desktop` using the command `cp -p $(realpath workbench.desktop) workbench-mesa.desktop`. Next, open the newly created `~/Desktop/workbench-mesa.desktop` file in a text editor (e.g., `nano ~/Desktop/workbench-mesa.desktop`) and change all instances of `runwb2` to `runwb2 -oglmesa`. Save the changes, then refresh the Jupyter desktop by pressing `control-R`. A new icon for Workbench (Mesa) should appear, which you can double-click to launch Workbench using Mesa for OpenGL calls.

qa_pairs.question: When experiencing graphics corruption in Ansys Fluent launched from within Workbench on a Jupyter Lab Desktop (without a GPU), what specific environment variable can be set to address this?
qa_pairs.answer: If graphics appear unusable or corrupted when launching Ansys Fluent from within Workbench on a Jupyter Lab Desktop (without a GPU), you can try setting the environment variable `HOOPS_PICTURE=opengl2-mesa`. This setting should be copied and pasted into the Environment Tab of the Fluent Launcher selector panel that appears when Fluent is started from Workbench.
